content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Kereli Sengstack 
FOTH President 
1 W. Campbell Ave. 
Campbell, CA 95008 
Email: foththeatre@gmail.com 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
· Our File No. A-19-058 
Dear Ms. Sengstack: 
June 10, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the Act). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of 
interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as 
common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact 
when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes 
your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions 
should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Should the members of the Friends of the Heritage Theater (FOTH) be listed as "designated 
employees" by the City of Campbell (the City or Campbell) required to file Statements of 
Economic Interests (Form 700) under the Act? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. As "consultants" to the City, FOTH members must be included within the City's 
conflict of interest code as "designated employees" and required to file a Form 700 disclosure 
statement under the Act, as directed by the City. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
The Heritage Theater is an 800-seat Theater located in the City on the grounds of the 
Campbell Recreation Center (the Theater). The City owns and operates the Theater and other 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-19-058 
Page No. 2 
Recreation Center facilities. The FOTH is a 50l(c)(3) non-profit charitable organization, formed in 
October 1998 to: 
1. Promote the use of the Theater as a performing arts Theater; 
2. Ensure that the Theater is a resource for activities to benefit the youth of Campbell as 
well as youth of the entire region; 
3. Ensure that the Theater is a resource for the cultural enrichment of the Campbell 
community and the entire region; 
4. Raise funds to preserve and benefit the Theater outside of the City's purveyances; 
5. Raise funds to support the use of the Theater as a vehicle to promote downtown 
Campbell and the cultural growth of the community, and to support the ongoing 
operation of the Theater as a performing arts venue; and 
6. Educate the community as to the history of the Theater and the historical significance of 
the building. 
Pursuant to the FOTH bylaws, its Board of Directors is composed of community volunteers. 
Prospective Board members are elected by the current Board, and officer positions of President, 
Vice President, Secretary and Treasurer are elected to serve two-year terms. The City does not 
participate in or influence the election of FOTH board members or officers. The Theater manager, a 
City liaison, and a representative of Venue Tech, the company the City has contracted to help 
manage and operate the Theater, frequently attend FOTH board meetings for informational 
purposes only. The FOTH has no contract with the City to use the Theater. 
In mid-February, 2019, certain members of the FOTH Board of Directors received an e-mail 
from the Campbell City Clerk requesting they complete and submit a Form 700. The City had not 
notified the FOTH to complete a Form 700 in at least 15 years. 
After inquiring about the requirement, the FOTH learned that in 2002, pursuant to 
confidential legal advice from the City Attorney, the City designated the FOTH as a "Designated 
Employee" in Appendix A of the City's Conflict oflnterest Code. 
You state that the City has informed FOTH that the 2002 decision to include members of 
FOTH as "designated employees" was made in light of the fact that VenueTech is a for-profit 
company contracted by the City to run the Heritage Theater's operations, marketing and budget. 
VenueTech established the FOTH in 1998. Accordingly, the FOTH has been managing the 
operations at the City owned theater, under the authority ofVenueTech's long term contract with 
the City, for over 20 years. While you also state that the FOTH does not receive funds from the 
City, it does appear that public funds are received by VenueTech, which established the FOTH to 
perform its obligations under its contract with the City.2 
2 We note that. you have also indicated that the FOTH is not treated as a public entity under other bodies of 
law. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-19-058 
Page No. 3 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their 
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial 
interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Consequently, every agency 
must adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest code. (Section 87300.) 
A conflict of interest code must be formulated at the most decentralized level possible 
(Section 87301); 3 enumerate the positions within the agency, including consultant positions, that 
involve making or participation in the making of decisions which may have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on any financial interest (Section 87302(a)); and set forth the 
specific types of investments, business positions, real property interests, or sources of income which 
are reportable for each enumerated position (ibid). Public officials that have enumerated within an 
agency's conflict of interest code are collectively references as "designated employees."4 
A. 
Public Official. 
The term "public official" includes "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a 
state or local government agency." (Section 82048 [ emphasis added]; Regulation 18700.) The issue 
is whether the FOTH or its members are "consultants" under the Act. Regulation 18700.3(a)(2) 
defines a "consultant" to include an individual who, pursuant to a contract with an agency, does the 
following: 
Serves in a staff capacity with the agency and in that capacity participates in making a 
governmental decision as defined in Regulation 18704(a) and (b) or performs the same or 
substantially all the same duties for the agency that would otherwise be performed by an 
individual holding a position specified in the agency's Conflict of Interest Code under 
Section 87302. 
B. 
Consultant. 
1. 
Serves in a staff capacity. 
To determine "staff capacity," the existence of an ongoing relationship between the 
contractor and the public agency is significant. We have previously found that a contractor serves in 
a staff capacity when the contract calls.for work to be performed "over more than on~ year" on 
"high level" projects (Ennis Advice Letter, No. A-15-006; see also Ferber Advice Letter, No. A-98-
118). We have also advised that a contractor does not act in a staff capacity where the work is to be 
performed on one project or a limited number of projects over a limited period of time (Sanchez 
Advice Letter, No. A-97-438); where the relationship between the contractor and the agency would 
last only 12-16 months with no ongoing relationship contemplated (Harris Advice Letter, No. A-
3 Because the determination of whether an individual is a public official subject to disclosure is best made by 
the employing agency, we generally defer to the agency's determination unless there is reason to suggest that the 
determination is incorrect. 
4 Accordingly, the term "designated employees" encompasses more than just rank and file all public official 
extending to all public official's enumerated in the agency's code. 


File No. A-19-058 
Page No. 4 
02-239); and where, under a multi-year contract, the contractor would perform only on a sporadic 
basis. (Maze Advice Letter, No. I-95-296; Parry Advice Letter, No. I-95-064.) 
In this case, the City contracted with VenueTech to handle the Theater's operations, 
marketing and budget in or around 1998. VenueTech established the FOTH in 1998. Since then, the 
FOTH has conducted its business and operations in a City-owned Theater under the authority of 
VenueTech's contract with the City. Although the FOTH receives no direct City funding, it has 
been managing the operations of the City-owned Theater for more than 20 years. The FOTH use of 
the Theater is ongoing, not temporary. The FOTH's relationship with the City began years ago, 
when the FOTH began using the City-owned Theater, and in 2002 when the City identified the 
FOTH as a "designated employee" in its conflict of interest code. 
The City hired VenueTech to perform duties at the Theater that City staff would otherwise 
be required to perform to operate and manage the Theater. The FOTH and VenueTech have similar 
scopes of duties at the Theater. For instance, the FOTH handles various aspects of the Theater's 
operations, marketing and budget. Consequently, the FOTH and its members serve in a staff 
capacity for the City and are "consultants" under Regulation 18700.3(b )(2). 
The facts provided establish that the FOTH and its members meet the definition under 
Regulation 18700.3(b )(2) because they serve in a staff capacity for the City. The Commission has 
construed the phrase "serves in a staff capacity" in Regulation 18700.3(b )(2) to include ·individuals 
who are performing substantially all the same tasks that normally would be performed by one or 
more staff members of a governmental agency. 
2. 
- Participates in making a governm·ental decision. 
In addition to serving in a staff capacity, a "consultant" must "participate in making a 
governmental decision, which includes a public official's authorization or direction of any action by 
an agency." (See Regulation 18704(a).) "Participating in a decision" occurs when the official 
"provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision 
without significant intervening substantive review." (Regulation 18704(b ).) "Significant intervening 
substantive review" has been interpreted to require more than the mere review of the 
recommendations by superiors, but rather the independent checking of the results without solely 
relying on the data of the official. (Greenwald Advice Letter, No. I-90-349.) 
Here, the FOTH is making decisions about use of the City-owned Theater. While the City 
does not directly provide FOTH funding, the FOTH is managing a public asset and is doing so 
without significant substantive review of the City. Accordingly, as an organization that uses, 
operates and promotes the City-owned Theater, the FOTH participates in making governmental 
decisions under Regulation 18700.3(a)(2). 
Based on the facts provided, the members of FOTH are "consultants" under the Act and, as 
directed by the City, must file Statements of Economic Interests as "designated employees" of the 
City. 


File No. A-19-058 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JMFjr: aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge

statement under the Act, as directed by the City.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Should the members of the Friends of the Heritage Theater (FOTH) be listed as "designated 
employees" by the City of Campbell (the City or Campbell) required to file Statements of 
Economic Interests (Form 700) under the Act?

CONCLUSION: Yes. As "consultants" to the City, FOTH members must be included within the City's 
conflict of interest code as "designated employees" and required to file a Form 700 disclosure 
statement under the Act, as directed by the City.
